metadata.summary: Lambda cold start causes, runtime-specific impact, code-level patterns, and mitigation techniques: provisioned concurrency, SnapStart, package size, ARM/Graviton.

content.title: Serverless Cold Start Optimization
content.summary: Cold starts occur when a new Lambda function instance is created (no warm container exists). Mitigation ranges from code-level patterns (init outside handler, smaller packages) to infrastructure choices (provisioned concurrency, SnapStart, ARM/Graviton). The correct fix depends on the runtime, traffic pattern, and latency SLO.

why.p: Cold starts add unpredictable latency to user-facing requests and can violate p95/p99 SLOs. Java and .NET runtimes suffer 1-5 second cold starts that make them unsuitable for synchronous APIs without mitigation. Even Python and Node.js 100-500ms cold starts are significant for interactive UIs. Understanding the cause enables the cheapest fix rather than defaulting to expensive provisioned concurrency.

list.item: Cold start rate exceeds 10% for user-facing Lambda functions.
p95 latency spikes are traced to cold start events in X-Ray or Lumigo.
Adopting Java, .NET, or large Python/Node packages in a Lambda that must respond in <2s.
Evaluating whether provisioned concurrency ROI justifies the cost for a given function.
Background processing functions (Kinesis consumers, SQS workers) where latency variance is irrelevant — cold starts do not affect user experience.
Batch or scheduled jobs — cold start is a one-time cost amortized over the batch duration.
Functions already using provisioned concurrency that are meeting their SLOs — further optimization has diminishing returns.

section.name: cold-start-by-runtime
section.p: Typical cold start durations by runtime and optimization priority (2025 benchmarks, 512MB memory):

list.item: Python: 200-500ms — Medium priority; eliminate heavy imports at handler scope.
Node.js: 100-300ms — Low priority; already fast for most use cases.
Go: 50-150ms — Low priority; compiled binary with minimal runtime overhead.
Rust: 50-150ms — Low priority; compiled binary, fastest cold starts available.
.NET: 500ms-2s — High priority; enable SnapStart (Native AOT or .NET 8+).
Java: 1-5s — Critical; enable SnapStart (Java 11+) or migrate to GraalVM native compilation.

section.name: optimization-techniques
section.p: Optimization techniques ranked by effectiveness and cost (best to worst ROI):

list.item: Init code outside handler — 20-40% reduction, free. Moves AWS client initialization and heavy imports to module scope so warm invocations skip them entirely.
Smaller packages — 10-30% reduction, free. Remove unused dependencies, use tree-shaking, strip test/dev deps from deployment package.
Native compilation (GraalVM, Native AOT) — 50-80% reduction, free. Eliminates JVM/CLR startup entirely. Requires build pipeline change.
ARM/Graviton — 10-20% faster startup and 20% cheaper per GB-second. Change Architectures: [arm64] — no code change for Python/Node/Go.
Right-size memory — more memory = more CPU = faster startup. Use AWS Lambda Power Tuning to find the optimal memory/cost/latency balance.
Keep-Warm Pings — moderate effectiveness, $ cost. EventBridge rule fires every 5 minutes; not reliable under concurrent load.
SnapStart (Java 11+/.NET 8+) — ~90% reduction, free. AWS Lambda takes a snapshot of the initialized execution environment and restores from it.
Provisioned Concurrency — eliminates cold starts entirely, $$$$ cost. Pay for always-on instances regardless of traffic. Only justified for latency-critical paths with measurable SLO violations.

rule.statement: AWS SDK clients, database connections, HTTP clients, and model files MUST be initialized at module scope (outside the handler function). Imports that take >10ms MUST also be at module scope.
rule.rationale: Module scope runs once per container. Handler scope runs on every invocation, adding initialization latency to every request instead of only the first cold start.

rule.statement: Lambda deployment packages MUST exclude test dependencies, documentation, and unused code. Python packages MUST use --no-deps for transitive imports that are already in the runtime. Node packages MUST use tree-shaking (esbuild or webpack) with target=esm.
rule.rationale: Package size directly correlates with cold start time. The Lambda loader must unzip and initialize the deployment package on cold start. Every MB saved reduces cold start proportionally.

rule.statement: Java and .NET Lambda functions serving synchronous user-facing requests MUST enable SnapStart (Java 11+ or .NET 8+ with Native AOT). SnapStart MUST be combined with a post-deploy warm-up invocation to pre-populate the snapshot cache.
rule.rationale: JVM and CLR startup dominates Java and .NET cold starts (1-5s). SnapStart restores from a pre-initialized snapshot in <200ms without provisioned concurrency cost.

rule.statement: All new Lambda functions MUST default to ARM/Graviton (Architectures: [arm64]) unless the runtime or dependency explicitly requires x86. Existing functions MUST be migrated to ARM on the next deployment that changes the function code.
rule.rationale: ARM/Graviton delivers 10-20% faster cold starts and 20% lower GB-second cost with no code changes for Python, Node.js, and Go. The only exception is native extensions compiled for x86.

example.title: Module-scope client initialization
example.note: Boto3 client creation involves TLS negotiation with IAM endpoint. At 100 requests/second this saves ~50ms per warm invocation.

example.title: Handler-scope client initialization
example.note: Moves import + client creation inside the handler. At 200ms per cold-start equivalent added to every warm invocation, this breaks any p95 latency SLO.

example.title: ML model cached outside handler
example.code:
# Load model outside handler (cached across warm invocations) import onnxruntime as ort import boto3 import os
# Download and cache model on cold start model_path = '/tmp/model.onnx' if not os.path.exists(model_path): s3 = boto3.client('s3') s3.download_file('ml-models-bucket', 'model-v1.onnx', model_path)
session = ort.InferenceSession(model_path)
def handler(event, context): input_data = preprocess(event['body']) result = session.run(['output'], {'input': input_data}) return {'statusCode': 200, 'body': str(result[0].tolist())}
example.note: Model download happens once per container (cold start). Warm invocations reuse the in-memory ONNX session. /tmp is preserved across warm invocations within the same container.

example.title: SAM template with SnapStart and ARM
example.code: Resources: InferenceFunction: Type: AWS::Serverless::Function Properties: Handler: inference.handler Runtime: python3.11 MemorySize: 3008 # More memory = more CPU for faster init Timeout: 30 Architectures: - arm64 # 20% cheaper, often faster startup ProvisionedConcurrencyConfig: ProvisionedConcurrentExecutions: 5 # Keep 5 instances always warm Environment: Variables: MODEL_VERSION: v1
example.note: Use provisioned concurrency only for latency-critical paths where cost is justified. Use AWS Lambda Power Tuning to validate the memory setting before committing.

section.name: agent-prompts
section.prompt:
Analyze and optimize cold starts for this Lambda function:
Runtime: [Python/Node.js/Java/etc.] Current cold start: [X ms] Target cold start: [X ms] Package size: [X MB] Memory: [X MB] VPC: [yes/no]
Dependencies: [List main dependencies]
Please analyze: 1. Main contributors to cold start time 2. Quick wins (no code changes) 3. Code-level optimizations 4. Dependency optimization 5. Provisioned concurrency recommendation 6. SnapStart eligibility (if Java/.NET)

Optimize Lambda deployment package:
Current size: [X MB] Runtime: [Python/Node.js/etc.]
Dependencies (from requirements.txt/package.json): [list dependencies]
Please recommend: 1. Dependencies to remove or replace 2. Tree-shaking configuration 3. Layer strategy 4. Native dependency handling 5. Expected size reduction

checklist.check: Move all AWS client initialization and heavy imports to module scope outside handler.
Check cold start rate in CloudWatch Metrics or Lambda Insights (target <10%).
Enable ARM/Graviton (arm64) for all new functions; migrate existing on next code change.
Optimize package size: remove unused deps, enable tree-shaking, use Lambda layers for shared code.
Enable SnapStart for Java 11+ and .NET 8+ functions on synchronous user-facing paths.
Evaluate provisioned concurrency only after all free optimizations are exhausted and SLO is still violated.
Run AWS Lambda Power Tuning to find optimal memory/latency/cost balance.
Verify external HTTP calls have explicit timeouts shorter than the function timeout — never inherit function timeout as HTTP timeout.

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: Common agent mistakes for cold start optimization:

list.item: Cold-start timing in LLM answers is anecdotal; route any latency claim to aws-lambda-power-tuning rather than trusting LLM numbers.
Generated Layers: ARNs are often wrong (region/version drift). Always parameterize via SSM Public Parameters or fetch the latest ADOT layer in CI.
LLMs default to recommending provisioned concurrency first — it is the most expensive option. Always attempt code-level and package-size optimizations first.
